title: Unit 1 Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass: The Greek Writing System

script.text: Welcome... to the Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass for Unit 1: The Greek Writing System. If you've ever looked at a Greek sentence and felt like it was code you couldn't crack... today is the day we change that. Greek isn't just an alphabet; it's the foundation of over 25% of the English words you use every day. I'm William, and I'm joined by Athina, our native phonetic expert. Athina... how should a beginner approach the Greek script?

My advice is simple: don’t try to memorize it like a random list of 24 symbols. Think of it as a logical evolution. Many letters are exactly like English... some are 'False Friends'... and some are the unique 'True Greeks'. We will master them all, step by step.

Exactly. Let's start with our best friends... Group 1: The Twins. These letters are your safety net. They look and sound almost exactly like their English counterparts. Athina... let's hear them clearly.
Α, Άλφα... Ε, Έψιλον... Ι, Ιώτα... Κ, Κάππα... Μ, Μι... Ν, Νι... Ο, Όμικρον... Τ, Ταυ... Και Ζ, Ζήτα...
The sounds for these are straightforward: like 'a' in father, 'e' in pet, 'ee' in meet, 'k' in kite, 'm' in mom, 'n' in no, 'o' in not, 't' in top, and 'z' in zoo.
Now... we have to pause here for a critical 'Pro Tip'. In English, when we say words like 'pot' or 'top'... we release a little puff of air. This is called aspiration. But in Greek... consonants like
are perfectly crisp and dry. Athina... can you show the difference?
Of course. In English, you might say 'Top'. In Greek... the
No air. It’s like the 'T' in 'stop' rather than 'top'.

That's a vital distinction. Keep it 'dry'. Now... let's tackle the dangerous ones. Group 2: The False Friends. These letters look familiar... but they are trying to trick you. Athina... alert the listeners.
looks like a 'B'... but it is ALWAYS a 'V'. Never a 'B'.
looks like an 'H'... but it is ALWAYS an 'ee' sound.
looks like a 'P'... but it is our rolled 'R'.
looks like an 'X'... but it is a soft 'H' or the Scottish 'Loch'. And
looks like a 'Y'... but it is another 'ee' sound.

So... to summarize: B is for Van... H is for Meet... P is for Roll... X is for Loch... and Y is for Meet again. Rethink your brain's wiring! Now... let’s meet the icons. Group 3: The True Greeks. These are the shapes you see in math and science. Athina... take us through them.
Γάμμα... Δέλτα... Θήτα... Λάμδα... Ξι... Πι... Σίγμα... Φι... Ψι... Ωμέγα.
Let’s look at the tricky ones.
is special. Before 'a', 'o', or 'ou'... it’s a soft 'gh' sound. But before 'e' or 'i'... it sounds like the 'y' in 'yes'. Listen:
They are very different sounds in Greek.
the letter with a double identity. Why does it change shape?
Sigma is the 'S' sound. In the middle of a word... it’s a circle with a tail:
But if it is the very last letter... it looks like a

Excellent. Section 2: The Digraphs. This is the real 'Masterclass' level. Greek is famous for having multiple ways to write the same sound... especially the 'ee' sound. In fact... there are six! Athina... why so many 'ee's?
It’s a gift from History! Long ago... they all sounded different. But in Modern Greek... they all sound like
It keeps us on our toes. And then we have the 'Chameleon Vowels'. When
stops being a vowel and starts being a consonant. Athina... show us the two faces of the combinations.
This is a key rule. If followed by a soft sound...
But! If followed by a hard sound like

sound after it is voiceless. Fascinating. And finally... the 'New' consonants. Ancient Greek didn't have a 'B' or 'D' sound as we know them today. So... we had to build them. Athina... show us the builders.
To get a 'B' sound... we use
dad. To get a 'D' sound... we use
to dress. And for a 'G' sound... we use
your brain should read 'D'. If you see
read 'B'. It’s an instant conversion. Section 3: The Energy. Greek words have a rhythm... and that rhythm is guided by the
That little dash over a vowel. Athina... how important is it?
It is everything! In Greek... you must stress the correct syllable or the word might change meaning. The
tells you where the punch is. Listen to
for who or which. The accent mark is the only difference!
It’s the soul of the word. And lastly... punctuation. Most is the same as English... but the question mark...?
Yes... the Greek question mark is a semicolon! If you see
... it’s not a list... it’s a question. And our semicolon is just a dot in the air... the 'Upper Dot'.

You have now completed the Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass for Unit 1. You can identify letters... navigate false friends... decode digraphs... and correctly stress every word. This foundation will make everything else so much easier. Excellent work... and we will see you in the deep dive for Unit 2: Nouns and Articles.
Συγχαρητήρια! Είστε έτοιμοι για το επόμενο βήμα. Γεια σας!
